population_summary: consecutive adults undergoing coronary artery bypass surgery with preoperative sleep study
overall_reason: Abstract-level extraction only from an older specialized postoperative cohort; limited reporting detail and specialized surgical framing warrant high overall bias risk.

population_summary: adults aged 70 years or younger with verified coronary artery disease
sleep_study_type_summary: hospital overnight sleep study with oximetry and airflow
overall_reason: Prospective CAD cohort with adjusted cerebrovascular outcomes, but older design features, limited modern sleep-phenotyping detail, and CAD-specific selection constrain generalizability.

cohorts: elderly community subgroup with CVD and impaired systolic function
population_summary: community-dwelling elderly with cardiovascular disease and impaired systolic function by NT-proBNP
overall_reason: Community-based design is a strength, but the extracted association is confined to a high-risk subgroup, which reduces transportability and increases interpretation complexity.

population_summary: hospitalized adults with left ventricular systolic dysfunction after acute decompensated heart failure
overall_reason: Very specialized hospitalized heart-failure cohort with limited size and stepwise modeling, creating high confounding and selection concerns.

population_summary: chronic stable heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and mixed SDB phenotypes
overall_reason: Long follow-up and adjusted mortality modeling are strengths, but the mixed obstructive/central SDB heart-failure setting limits direct applicability to adult OSA prognosis.

population_summary: consecutively hospitalized adults with decompensated heart failure
overall_reason: Extremely small specialized cohort with wide confidence intervals and limited recoverable model detail, making the estimate fragile and high risk of bias.

population_summary: adults referred to a large academic hospital with suspected OSA and no baseline arrhythmia
overall_reason: Large clinically relevant cohort with hard AF outcome linkage, but suspected-OSA referral sampling and non-AHI-adjusted nocturnal-hypoxemia modeling warrant moderate caution.

population_summary: community-based older men; not restricted to OSA at entry; community-based middle-aged and older adults; not restricted to OSA at entry
overall_reason: Large well-characterized cohorts, adjudicated mortality outcomes, robust covariate adjustment including AHI/MinSat/TST90, and consistent reporting.

population_summary: community-based older men; not restricted to OSA at entry
overall_reason: Well-conducted community cohort analysis with objective nocturnal hypoxemia measures and AHI adjustment, but older-men-only sampling and decomposition of oxygen-burden components introduce moderate generalizability and comparability concerns.

population_summary: newly diagnosed moderate-to-severe OSA without concomitant respiratory disease
overall_reason: Single-centre threshold analysis with no multivariable adjustment and OR-based bivariate comparisons only.

population_summary: community-based middle-aged and older adults; effect estimated from male subgroup via sex interaction; community-based older men
analytic_n_summary: 2259 men subgroup; 4535 in fully adjusted interaction model; 2653; 2646 in fully adjusted model
overall_reason: Strong cohort design and adjusted survival models, with only minor reporting limitations around subgroup event counts.

population_summary: AF-free patients investigated for clinical suspicion of OSA
sleep_study_type_summary: mixed PSG and other diagnostic sleep studies
overall_reason: Large multicenter OSA-referred cohort with adjusted time-to-event models and clinically relevant AF outcomes, but referral-based sampling, mixed diagnostic sleep-study modalities, and retrospective linkage design introduce moderate bias risk.

cohorts: Pays de la Loire clinical cohort
population_summary: newly diagnosed clinical OSA without overt cardiovascular disease
overall_reason: Good clinical cohort with adjusted time-to-event models, but mixed PSG/HSAT acquisition and composite endpoint definition complicate direct synthesis.

population_summary: atrial fibrillation patients undergoing ambulatory sleep study before catheter ablation
overall_reason: Highly specialized post-ablation subgroup analysis with non-CPAP restriction and limited event count, yielding high susceptibility to selection and residual confounding.

population_summary: adults with cardiovascular risk factors undergoing major non-cardiac surgery and unrecognized OSA
overall_reason: Large perioperative cohort with relevant hard outcomes, but it represents an unrecognized-OSA surgical setting rather than chronic OSA prognosis and the text-layer covariate detail is incomplete.

population_summary: community-dwelling Japanese adults initially free of ischemic heart disease and stroke
overall_reason: Large community cohort with incident cardiovascular outcomes and adjusted Cox models, but abstract-only extraction and OSA-surrogate framing limit certainty.

outcome_families: incident hard CVD; all-cause mortality; incident CVD; CVD mortality
population_summary: community-based middle-aged and older adults; not restricted to OSA at entry; community-based older men; not restricted to OSA at entry
overall_reason: Outcomes and HB measurement are strong, but the final models emphasize comparative physiological burdens and do not uniformly adjust for other OSA severity measures such as AHI.

population_summary: clinical sleep-study patients without baseline AF
overall_reason: Large and clinically relevant cohort, but referral-based selection and outcome ascertainment through administrative/electronic records introduce moderate bias risk.

population_summary: community-based adults aged >40 years; not restricted to OSA at entry; community-based adults aged >40 years; baseline-prevalent CVD excluded for morbidity dataset
overall_reason: Useful comparative paper, but reconstructed HB and same-cohort metric comparison increase measurement and interpretation uncertainty.

population_summary: adults with OSA diagnosed in a referral sleep-disorders centre
overall_reason: Large clinical OSA cohort with polysomnography-derived ODI and multivariable mortality models, but retrospective single-centre referral sampling, electronic-record outcome ascertainment, and non-AHI-adjusted collinearity handling warrant moderate caution.

population_summary: community-based adults with diabetes; community-based adults with treated diabetes
overall_reason: Post hoc subgrouping with empirically selected cutoffs and incomplete subgroup-size reporting limits credibility despite adjusted mortality models.

population_summary: community-based adults with symptom data and high-quality oxygen signals, free of baseline CVD
overall_reason: Methodologically solid, but the analytic subset, overlap with prior SHHS publications, and dependence on alternate model structures warrant moderate caution.

population_summary: acute coronary syndrome patients with dual remnant-cholesterol and low-grade-inflammation risk
sleep_study_type_summary: sleep study in hospitalized ACS cohort
overall_reason: Modern subgroup analysis with relevant clinical outcome, but post hoc RCIR subgrouping and ACS-specific selection limit direct OSA prognostic generalizability.

population_summary: adults with moderate-to-severe OSA and coronary artery disease
sleep_study_type_summary: sleep study in coronary artery disease cohort
overall_reason: Clinically important coronary-disease OSA cohort with a positive HB-MACCE signal, but abstract-only extraction and secondary-prevention framing limit direct comparability to the core prognostic pool.

population_summary: adults referred for polysomnography in a Hong Kong sleep clinic
sleep_study_type_summary: in-laboratory polysomnography linked with territory-wide administrative outcomes
overall_reason: Large modern sleep-clinic cohort with hard cardiovascular outcomes and AHI-aware modeling, but single-center clinic sampling and spline-only effect presentation limit direct comparability.

population_summary: adults referred for sleep study with moderate-to-severe OSA and diabetes-comorbidity framing
overall_reason: Clinically relevant prospective cohort with an adjusted T90 cardiovascular-mortality signal, but diabetes-enriched phenotype framing and limited reporting detail keep overall certainty moderate.

cohorts: SHHS OSA without baseline AF cohort
population_summary: SHHS participants with OSA and no baseline atrial fibrillation
sleep_study_type_summary: home polysomnography in the Sleep Heart Health Study
overall_reason: Useful incident-AF analysis in a large SHHS-derived OSA cohort, but overlap-prone cohort reuse and OR-based modeling reduce comparability to the primary pooled architecture.

population_summary: adults with OSA undergoing major noncardiothoracic surgery
sleep_study_type_summary: preoperative diagnostic sleep study linked to administrative surgical outcomes
overall_reason: Large perioperative translational cohort with relevant hard outcomes, but surgical timing and postoperative-composite framing limit general chronic-OSA prognostic transportability.

population_summary: adults with recent myocardial infarction undergoing full-night polysomnography after the index event
sleep_study_type_summary: full-night polysomnography after recent myocardial infarction
overall_reason: Small specialized post-MI cohort with landmarked outcome modeling and limited event support; strong signal for nadir oxygen saturation is clinically interesting but highly context-dependent.

population_summary: community-dwelling men in the Sleep Heart Health Study
overall_reason: Important community-based stroke analysis with longitudinal follow-up, but older modeling conventions, sex-restricted inference, and wide confidence intervals warrant moderate caution.

cohorts: SHHS community cohort; SantOSA clinical cohort
population_summary: community SHHS adults and clinical SantOSA adults with complete baseline and follow-up data
sleep_study_type_summary: unattended home polysomnography; diagnostic sleep-study referral cohort
overall_reason: Dual-cohort T90 mortality paper with adjusted models in both a community SHHS cohort and a clinical SantOSA cohort, but single-publication dual-cohort structure and mixed population framing limit direct comparability.
